author: haiku-list-ready-monotone <t@t.com>
body:
## Findings

Shipped Property 6 `list_ready_monotone_under_status_flips` in
`crates/jjf-storage/tests/proptest_harness.rs`:

- Mutation enum + helpers: lines 542-635 (`ListReadyMutation`,
  strategies, `status_is_active`, `apply_list_ready_mutation`).
- Property body: lines 1052-1194 (inside the `proptest!` block).

The property unifies all three cases from the ticket into one
classifier on observed pre/post status (read from disk, not
predicted from verb — handles failed-mutation atomicity for free):

- `active → inactive` (Open/Blocked/InProgress → Closed/Abandoned):
  every other id's membership can only ADD; target itself MUST leave.
- `inactive → active`: every other id's membership can only REMOVE;
  target itself may newly appear.
- All other transitions (active↔active, no-op, status-preserving
  verbs like add_label/add_comment): symmetric difference ⊆ {target}.
  Captures that an `Open → Blocked` flip drops the target from the
  default `ReadyFilter` but doesn't touch any other issue's
  blocked-set membership.

## Recommendation

Use `Storage::list_ready(&ReadyFilter::default())` directly — the
storage API exists and is exactly what `cli::ready::run_ready`
calls underneath. No need to factor a new `ready_ids()` helper or
reach into the CLI layer.

The "active vs inactive" framing (mirroring `compute_blocked_set`'s
`is_active`) was the load-bearing insight — it cleanly handles
that `Blocked` issues are still "active" for dep purposes but
absent from default `ReadyFilter` output, which a naive
"closed/abandoned vs not" split would have gotten wrong.

## Confidence

High. Green at default 16 cases (33.6s in the workspace run) and
stress-clean at `PROPTEST_CASES=256` (321s for the single
property). Full harness wall-clock 25.43s release — well under
the 40s target. Full workspace 624 tests pass via
`cargo nextest run --workspace`.

## Open follow-ups

None for this property. Companion ticket
`proptest-cycle-rejection` (`1078439`) remains the place to
exercise the cycle-rejection codepath directly — this property
catches cycle-locked ids as a side effect (a cycle-locked id
stays missing from `list_ready` across a release that should
have added it), but doesn't assert the rejection itself.
